workPortion.label: Holthausen, Rezepte, Segen und Zaubersprüche aus zwei Stockholmer Handschriften
notes: Direct text and boundary inspection confirms the article title/opening on PDF page 89/printed p. 75 and the article ending on PDF page 102/printed p. 88.
Viewer leaf n89 begins printed page 75 of the article.

bibliographic.citation: Ferdinand Holthausen, ‘Rezepte, Segen und Zaubersprüche aus zwei Stockholmer Handschriften,’ Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 19 (N.F. 7) (1897): 75–88.
bibliographic.title: Rezepte, Segen und Zaubersprüche aus zwei Stockholmer Handschriften

entities.label: Holthausen 1897 edition
notes: Section I occupies printed pages 75–86 and contains items I.1–28 plus notes.
Section II begins on printed page 86 and contains three charms on pages 87–88 plus notes and a postscript.

rights.basis: The work was published in 1897, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works; U-M independently marks the CME item based on it No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: The 1897 edition, transcription, selection, and editorial notes

rights.basis: Holthausen died in 1956. Swedish Copyright Act section 43 gives the ordinary term through the end of the seventieth year after the author's death, yielding a cautious end date of 2026-12-31 for protectable contributions.
rights.component: Any selection, transcription choices, notes, or other editorial contribution that meets the applicable originality threshold
notes: This is a term calculation, not a conclusion that every transcription or editorial detail is sufficiently original to be protected.
On the ordinary calculation, any such term ends after 2026-12-31; re-review this record on or after 2027-01-01.
Other jurisdictions can apply different rules.

dateStatements.label: KB nominal catalogue dating
dateStatements.notes: This is the date printed on the older nominal catalogue card.
dateStatements.value: second half of the fourteenth century

dateStatements.label: Holthausen palaeographic caveat
dateStatements.notes: Holthausen reports Stephens's fourteenth-century dating but says the script could equally belong to the fifteenth century.

entities.description: The principal manuscript witness for Holthausen's Section I. Its manuscript leaves use page numbering, so this record deliberately cites pages rather than folios.

holding.notes: The current public catalogue punctuation is X 90.

entities.label: Kungliga biblioteket X 90
notes: The public catalogue card also records a microfilm negative, MfH 428.
No public online manuscript facsimile was found in the KB Digitalt or Manuscripta shelfmark searches recorded below as of 2026-07-11.
Holthausen describes several scribal hands and changes of ink and paper.
The manuscript contains medical and cosmetic recipes, a Latin-English plant list, charms in several languages, and two longer English medical poems.
physicalDescription.support: Paper except for two parchment leaves

rights.basis: The manuscript text dates from the fourteenth or fifteenth century and is far outside any U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: The anonymous medieval textual content of the manuscript
notes: This statement concerns the old textual content, not modern photographs, scans, catalogue prose, transcriptions, or provider terms.

access.accessMethod: Ask KB to identify the current shelfmark using the historical designation and the 1847 catalogue citation before ordering access or copies
access.entityLabel: KB manuscript historically designated Miscellany VII
notes: Quote the description on Stephens 1847 page 25 and Holthausen Section II; do not order it as X 83.

entities.description: The separate Stockholm manuscript from which Holthausen printed the three charms in Section II. Its current shelfmark and present survival status have not been confirmed in an authoritative current catalogue.

holding.notes: The historical designation is well supported, but the current shelfmark must be confirmed by KB. Do not substitute X 83; that shelfmark describes an unrelated Andreas Drossander manuscript.

entities.label: KB manuscript historically designated Miscellany VII
notes: Holthausen places the recipe and charms near the opening of the manuscript but supplies no stable modern folio or page reference.
The manuscript formerly belonged to Christian Ravius, whose name Holthausen renders Christ. Ray.
Holthausen describes a miscellany written by multiple hands, with empty and partly or wholly torn leaves.

Contents described by Holthausen include notes on the 1596 Essex expedition to Cadiz aboard The John & Francis, prayers attributed to Elizabeth I, religious and political material, a mercury recipe, and the three charms.

rights.basis: The manuscript is dated 1597 and its content is far outside any U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: The 1597 manuscript's underlying textual content
notes: This statement does not cover a modern reproduction or a modern scholarly edition.

bibliographic.citation: George Stephens, ‘Extracts in Prose and Verse from an Old English Medical Manuscript, Preserved in the Royal Library at Stockholm,’ Archaeologia 30 (1844): 349–418; separately issued, London: J. B. Nichols and Son, 1844.
bibliographic.title: Extracts in Prose and Verse from an Old English Medical Manuscript, Preserved in the Royal Library at Stockholm

entities.label: Stephens 1844 prior edition
notes: Holthausen cites a separate offprint whose internal pagination differs from the Archaeologia pagination.
The same three charms later printed in Holthausen Section II occur on separate-offprint pages 50–51, corresponding to Archaeologia pages 398–399.
Holthausen states that his starred Section I items 1, 18, 19, 20, 22, and 28 were not printed by Stephens.

rights.basis: The work was published in 1844, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: The 1844 edition, transcription, and editorial text

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item CME00099, a keyed digital encoding of Ferdinand Holthausen's 1897 Anglia edition. Holthausen's Section I transcribes selected passages from Kungliga biblioteket X 90; Section II transcribes three charms from a separate 1597 Stockholm manuscript then designated Miscellany VII. George Stephens's 1844 edition is a prior edition that Holthausen consulted and re-collated against the manuscripts, not the sole direct source.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous contributors
catalogSummary.date: Source texts attested in a later-fourteenth- to fifteenth-century witness and a manuscript dated 1597; edited selection published 1897
catalogSummary.region: Section I: South-East Midlands dialect, with most X 90 main-hand passages localized to Norfolk; Section II origin unknown
catalogSummary.title: Rezepte, Segen und Zaubersprüche aus zwei Stockholmer Handschriften

catalogingSubject.label: Rezepte, Segen und Zaubersprüche aus zwei Stockholmer Handschriften
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The intellectual and content unit selected, edited, and published by Ferdinand Holthausen in 1897 and represented as CME00099: German editorial material plus 31 historical source-text excerpts. It is not either complete manuscript witness, the U-M digital encoding, or the repository XML file.

contentParts.label: Editorial frame and apparatus

locators.value: title; Section I and II prefaces; Anmerkungen; Nachschrift; closing

notes: This is a conceptual cataloging part; the editorial material is not one physically contiguous block.
contentParts.summary: German title, introductions to both manuscript selections, footnotes and commentary, section notes, postscript, and closing dateline/signature by Holthausen.

contentParts.label: Section I
contentParts.summary: Twenty-eight edited selections from manuscript X 90, dominated by prose medical recipes and charms with some verse.

contentParts.label: Section II

locators.value: three charms near the manuscript opening; no verified current page or folio locator

contentParts.summary: Three verse charms from the separate manuscript historically designated Miscellany VII and dated 1597.

assertion.notes: The claim being confirmed is the existence of these differing historical datings, not a single resolved date.

dateStatements.display: Attested in X 90, dated by the older KB catalogue to the second half of the fourteenth century; Holthausen says the script could instead be fifteenth-century
notes: This is a witness/attestation range, not the composition date of every selected text.

dateStatements.display: X 90 main hand: first quarter of the fifteenth century in LALME/MEG-C; MED citation ?before 1450
notes: This modern dating refines most, but not necessarily every, Section I passage.

scope.description: Passages written by X 90's main hand; does not include the different hands around manuscript pages 91–104

notes: The manuscript date does not prove that the three charms were first composed in 1597.

extent.description: Section I contains 28 items: 26 prose-only, one verse-only, and one mixed prose-and-verse item. Section II contains three verse-only items. Counts describe the Holthausen edition as encoded in CME00099, not necessarily the manuscripts' visual lineation.

formStatements.description: The edited selection contains both prose and verse, with prose dominant.
German introductions, commentary, notes, and postscript.
Twenty-six prose-only items, one verse-only item, and one item mixing prose and verse.
Three verse charms encoded as line groups with fifteen lines total.

scope.description: Embedded charms, prayers, verses, names, and formulae

languageStatements.label: French
notes: The XML root's LANG attribute omits French even though the item and Holthausen's description establish its presence.

assertion.notes: Using eng distinguishes the copied-1597 Section II language from the dominant Middle English of Section I, but the charms may preserve older linguistic material.

notes: This descriptive record intentionally separates intellectual authorship from editorship and digital encoding.
Stockholm is a manuscript holding location and Halle is an edition publication place; neither is used as the source texts' origin region here.
Historical medical and magical practices described by the source texts are not modern medical advice.
The tag set describes material actually selected in CME00099, not every work mentioned in Holthausen's descriptions of the complete manuscripts.

next_steps: Consult item-level scholarly editions, the Index of Middle English Prose, and charm or recipe analogues.
Create item-level date statements only where composition or earlier attestation evidence exists.
notes: The current date summary intentionally labels 1350–1499 and 1597 as witness attestations.
openQuestions.question: When were the 31 individual source texts originally composed, as distinct from the dates of the surviving manuscript witnesses?

next_steps: Research the 31 items individually before replacing anonymous authorship with more specific attributions.
openQuestions.question: Can any individual recipe, charm, blessing, prayer, or prognostic be attributed to a named author, compiler, translator, or earlier textual tradition?

next_steps: Identify the witness's current shelfmark and consult a modern description or linguistic analysis.
Compare the charms with earlier textual analogues before treating 1597 as their composition date or eng as a definitive language classification.
openQuestions.question: What are the place of origin, dialect, and most appropriate historical language classification for Section II's three copied-1597 English charms?

next_steps: Obtain a manuscript facsimile and consult a specialist linguistic description before using a narrower code or regional label.
openQuestions.question: What precise historical variety and date should be assigned to the French of Item I.24?

next_steps: Add item-level records only when stable identifiers and sufficient authorship, date, genre, and analogue research are available.
Until then, retain content_structure_status=summary and the two section-level parts.

assertion.notes: Confirmed as Holthausen's stated linguistic classification; confidence is medium as a current localization.

notes: Dialect region is not automatically identical to place of composition.
This is a scribal linguistic localization, not proof of where each underlying recipe or charm was composed.

scope.description: The X 90 main-hand passages represented in Section I; excludes other hands around manuscript pages 91–104 and all of Section II

assertion.notes: Holthausen does not attribute the individual source texts to named authors; multiple historical creators or transmitters are likely.

notes: This responsibility must not be reassigned to Holthausen merely because U-M's catalog interface labels him as Author.

scope.description: The 31 historical recipes, charms, blessings, prayers, and prognostics
Selection and organization of material from two manuscripts

assertion.notes: Holthausen explicitly says the Section I pieces were published after a new collation.

summaries.value: An edited selection of 31 anonymous historical texts from two Stockholm manuscript witnesses, comprising medical and herbal recipes, healing and protective charms, blessings, prayers, and prognostics. The source texts are predominantly English with embedded Latin and French; Ferdinand Holthausen supplies German introductions, textual notes, commentary, and a postscript.

Twenty-eight selections from X 90 concerning childbirth and fetal death, medical and reproductive prognosis, favor magic, garlic and venom, eye disease, toothache, epilepsy, fever, rosemary, bleeding, jaundice, wounds, gout and festering flesh, pregnancy, spleen treatment, fertility, and protective ritual.
Three verse charms from the manuscript dated 1597: a blood-staunching charm, a fever charm, and a spirit-expulsion or exorcism charm.

The introductions mention other material in the complete manuscripts—including cosmetic recipes, longer medical poems, a plant list, and the Cadiz-expedition diary—that Holthausen did not include in this article. Those subjects are not tagged as CME00099 content.

notes: This is Holthausen's editorial publication title, not a title supplied by either manuscript for the 31 pieces as a collective work.
titles.value: Rezepte, Segen und Zaubersprüche aus zwei Stockholmer Handschriften

assertion.notes: A project-supplied English translation for discovery, not a published alternate title.

titles.value: Recipes, Blessings, and Charms from Two Stockholm Manuscripts
Rezepte, segen und zaubersprüche aus zwei Stockholmer handschriften / [ed. F. Holthausen].

work.author: Anonymous contributors
work.dateDisplay: Source texts attested in a later-fourteenth- to fifteenth-century witness and a manuscript dated 1597; edited selection published 1897

work.regionDisplay: Section I: South-East Midlands dialect, with most X 90 main-hand passages localized to Norfolk; Section II origin unknown
work.summary: An edited selection of 31 anonymous historical texts from two Stockholm manuscript witnesses, comprising medical and herbal recipes, healing and protective charms, blessings, prayers, and prognostics. The source texts are predominantly English with embedded Latin and French; Ferdinand Holthausen supplies German introductions, textual notes, commentary, and a postscript.
